exemplar.title: Investigation on how Shallow and Deep Processing affects Memory Recall

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigation on how Shallow and Deep Processing affects Memory Recall

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and relevant aim with explained educational significance
Comprehensive description of Levels of Processing theory linked to the investigation
Operationalized IV and DV with concrete question examples and scoring method
weaknesses: Repetitive discussion of educational relevance could be more concise
Minor theoretical inaccuracy (colour vs. case) when describing shallow processing
Aim statement cuts off mid-sentence and could be more polished
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place the aim and relevance in band 5â6, theory/model in band 5â6 (with minor inaccuracies), and hypothesis/variables at band 6. The slight repetition and minor errors limit a top-band 6 rating overall, resulting in a holistic score of 5.

strengths: Research design choice explained with clear rationale against order effects
Convenience sampling justified with participant characteristics detailed
Controlled variables and materials described with thoughtful rationale
weaknesses: Link between specific controls and the confounds they mitigate could be clearer
Randomization procedure for group assignment lacks detail
External validity limitations of convenience sampling not discussed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three exploration strands meet band 3â4 descriptors: research design and sampling are well explained (band 4) and variables/materials are explained but unevenly developed (band 3). Overall performance aligns with the top of the band, meriting 4 marks.

strengths: Bar graph correctly formatted with error bars and directly addresses the hypothesis
Appropriate use and explanation of the Mann-Whitney U test
Interpretation clearly links statistical findings to the hypothesis and data
weaknesses: Minor formatting issues in graph (text overlap, lengthy title)
Overreliance on error-bar interpretation rather than inferential statistics
Exact p-value and sample size details are not reported
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Descriptive and inferential statistics are appropriately and accurately applied (band 5â6), and findings interpretation is clearly linked to data (band 5â6). Small presentation flaws and missing details prevent a top-band 6, so a score of 5 is awarded.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are explained and linked to validity/reliability
Modifications are explicitly tied to limitations with logical justification
Findings are discussed in relation to the Levels of Processing theory
weaknesses: Discussion of findings lacks depth and alternative explanations
Environmental distraction limitation not tied to outcome impact
Theoretical implications and broader educational significance are underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strengths/limitations and modifications meet band 5â6 descriptors, but the brief findings discussion fits band 3â4. Balancing these, a holistic score of 4 reflects overall evaluation performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph with error bars, clearly labelled axes, appropriate scale and condition labels, directly addresses the hypothesis.  Minor formatting issues (text overlap in the transcript) do not undermine correctness.  Hence band 5-6 is satisfied; a mid-band 5 reflects these small presentation flaws.

The student compares group means, comments on SDs, refers to overlapping error bars, and links the Mann-Whitney result to rejecting the null.  The interpretation is **clearly connected to the data and hypotheses**.  Some inferential terminology could be deeper (exact p not given), but the essential link is correct, so band 5-6 with 5 marks is appropriate.

Both null and alternative hypotheses are stated.  The IV (type of question: deep vs shallow) and DV (number of words recalled from a 10-item list) are **clearly operationalised** with concrete examples of the exact questions used and the scoring method (list of ten words).  This fully meets the highest descriptor; no omissions are evident, so the top of the band (6) is justified.

Convenience sampling is identified and **explicitly justified** (shared schedules, accessible location, time constraints).  Participant characteristics (age, grade, language, prior psychology knowledge) are thoroughly detailed and linked to the research purpose.  This constitutes an explanation rather than a mere description, meriting band 3-4 at 4 marks.

Levels of Processing theory is **clearly described** (structural vs semantic, depth determines LTM encoding) and the classic Craik & Tulving experiment is outlined.  The student then **links the theory to the present investigation** by asserting that semantic questions should improve recall in their high-school sample.  Minor inaccuracies (e.g., reference to colour rather than case) prevent the top of the band, so 5 is appropriate.

The findings are related back to the Levels of Processing theory and the original Craik & Tulving study, noting consistencies and methodological differences.  However, the discussion remains brief and lacks depth on theoretical implications or alternative explanations.  This aligns with a descriptive level with reference, fitting band 3-4 at 3 marks.

The student explicitly states the aim â âto understand how the levels of processing may affect memory retention for studentsâ â and then **explains its educational relevance** by linking the findings to study-skill strategies for teachers and pupils.  The potential benefit (better grades, improved memorization techniques) is elaborated rather than merely mentioned.  This satisfies the descriptor for band 5-6, though the explanation is fairly brief and somewhat repetitive, so a mid-band score of 5 is most fitting.

Multiple strengths (homogenous sample, independent measures, controlled setting) and limitations (low generalisability, convenience sampling, individual differences, environmental distractions) are **explained and linked to validity/reliability** of the investigation.  The coverage is thorough, earning band 5-6; minor repetition justifies 5 rather than 6.

Several controlled variables (slide formatting, exposure time, standardised instructions, venue, exclusion of IBDP psychology students) are listed **with short rationales**, and materials are justified (PowerPoint, pens, paper).  While the explanations are present, they are not equally developed for every control.  This comfortably fits band 3-4 but nearer the lower end, so 3 marks are assigned.

Proposed modifications (larger/random sample, wider age range, different timing/venue, researcher reflexivity) are **explicitly tied to the stated limitations** and justified in terms of improving generalisability, reducing bias and participant fatigue.  The linkage is clear and logical, satisfying band 5-6.  The justifications, while adequate, are not highly detailed, so 5 marks are appropriate.

The student not only describes that an independent-measures design was used but **explains why** (avoiding order effects, fatigue, practice, behaviour alteration).  This explanatory element places the work in band 3-4.  The rationale is sound but not highly analytical, warranting the upper mark of the band (4).

Means, standard deviations and a Mann-Whitney U test are reported.  The choice of test (non-parametric for two independent groups, small N) is appropriate and the critical comparison (U = 9 vs CV = 37, p â¤ 0.05) is accurately interpreted.  No calculation errors are visible, though raw ranks are absent; thus accuracy appears sound.  This meets band 5-6; a 5 is awarded as the statistical justification is adequate but not deeply elaborated.

annotations.comment: The paragraph on educational relevance repeats similar points; consider condensing to avoid redundancy.
The null hypothesis is clear but could be rephrased to explicitly reference measured outcomes and directionality.
Several controls are listed but their direct link to specific confounds is not always explicit; align each control with the variable it mitigates.
The interpretation states that overlapping error bars imply non-significance, which is not always valid; refer to inferential results instead.
Limitations are clearly identified and directly relevant to study validity, enhancing critical evaluation.
The Levels of Processing theory is clearly described with appropriate references to structural and semantic levels, demonstrating strong foundational understanding.
The description mentions âcolorâ as a shallow processing cue but the classic study used letter case; ensure accuracy when linking theory details.
The aim statement is introduced but cuts off mid-sentence; complete this to explicitly state both aim and expected outcome.
Independent and dependent variables are operationalized with concrete question examples and a ten-word recall scoring method, enhancing clarity.
Provide more detail on the randomization procedure for group assignment to strengthen the exploration of sampling validity.
The rationale for using an independent-measures design is well explained, effectively linking choices to control of order effects.
Convenience sampling is justified but the limitation for external validity is not addressed; discuss its impact on generalizability.
Participant characteristics (age range, language fluency, schooling context) are comprehensively detailed, supporting reproducibility.
The PowerPoint slides and standardized materials are well described, demonstrating thoughtful control over presentation variables.
The table lacks sample size (N) information for each group; include this to aid interpretation of descriptive statistics.
Explain why classifying data as ratio scale strengthens the choice of descriptive statistics in context.
The graph title is lengthy and could be streamlined to improve immediate clarity (e.g., âMean Words Recalled by Conditionâ).
The bar graph with error bars is correctly formatted and directly addresses the hypothesis for clear visual comparison.
Appropriate use and explanation of the two-tailed Mann-Whitney test demonstrates solid understanding of non-parametric analysis.
The discussion effectively ties results back to the Levels of Processing theory, reinforcing conceptual connections.
Expand on how differences in methodology (e.g., incidental vs. intentional learning) affect comparability with the original study.
Homogenous sampling is linked to increased internal validity, showing clear understanding of sampling implications.
The independent-measures design strength is well justified in terms of reducing order effects and demand characteristics.
Discuss how individual differences might quantitatively impact results (e.g., effect sizes) to deepen evaluation.
The environmental distraction limitation is described but not linked to outcome measurement; explain its likely effect on recall scores.
Proposed modifications are explicitly tied to limitations and justified, demonstrating thoughtful planning for future studies.
The conclusion accurately restates findings but would benefit from discussing broader educational implications or future research directions.
